PQC: Post-Quantum Cryptography. New math algorithms that quantum computers can't easily break.
Post-Quantum Cryptography: New math algorithms that quantum computers can't easily break.
Shor's Algorithm: A mathematical shortcut that allows a quantum computer to break modern encryption extremely fast.
Lattice-based: A type of math puzzle based on plotting points in a massive grid, used to build safe encryption.
Hash-based: A type of encryption that signs data securely by scrambling it multiple times.
Harvest Now, Decrypt Later: When hackers steal encrypted data today and store it until they have a quantum computer to crack it.
HNDL: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later — attackers steal encrypted data today to crack it when quantum computers arrive.
NIST: The US government agency responsible for writing and choosing the global standards for new algorithms.
Digital Signatures: Mathematical stamps that prove a message or file genuinely came from a specific person or server.
Quantum Computing: A new type of technology that uses quantum physics to solve complex problems millions of times faster than standard computers.
Quantum Computer: A new type of technology that uses quantum physics to solve complex problems millions of times faster than standard computers.
Classical Cryptography: The traditional math-based encryption we use today, like RSA or ECC.
Crypto Agility: The ability to quickly swap out old, broken encryption algorithms for new, secure ones without breaking the entire system.
Hybrid Cryptography: Using both old traditional math and new quantum-safe math at the same time to guarantee absolute safety during the transition.
ML-DSA: The new NIST-approved digital signature algorithm (FIPS 204) replacing RSA and ECDSA.
ML-KEM: The new NIST-approved key exchange algorithm (FIPS 203) for securely sharing secrets.
RSA: A widely-used encryption system based on multiplying large prime numbers — vulnerable to quantum attack.
TLS: Transport Layer Security — the protocol that puts the padlock in your browser's address bar.
CNSA 2.0: The US government's mandatory timeline requiring all national security systems to use quantum-safe algorithms by 2030.
CRQC: Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer — a future quantum machine powerful enough to break today's encryption.
CBOM: Cryptographic Bill of Materials — a complete inventory of every encryption algorithm used across an organization.
HSM: Hardware Security Module — a tamper-resistant physical device that stores and protects cryptographic keys.
SLH-DSA: A hash-based NIST signature standard (FIPS 205) used as a conservative backup when lattice math is a concern.
